content.text: A language model processes a sequence of tokens. An image encoder produces a sequence of dense vectors. Fusing the two means deciding: in what order do visual and text tokens appear, how does each attend to the other, and which weights are shared vs. separate? The answer shapes the model's memory cost, maximum context length, and ability to handle multiple images or interleaved multimodal content.

Three strategies cover the design space used by virtually all production multimodal LLMs. Each is a different answer to the same question: should the LLM see visual tokens in its regular attention stream, or should a dedicated cross-attention mechanism bring visual information in from the side?

attrs.alt: Three visual-text fusion strategies: token concatenation (LLaVA), cross-attention injection (Flamingo), and token interleaving (multi-image few-shot); with input format table by model

content.text: The simplest approach: visual tokens from the connector module are prepended to the text token sequence and the combined sequence is fed into the unmodified LLM. The LLM's standard self-attention operates over every token — both visual and text — in a single stream.

This is the architecture of LLaVA (all versions), BLIP-2 (with Q-Former output tokens), PaliGemma, and Qwen-VL. Its appeal is architectural simplicity: no changes to the LLM's internals, no new attention patterns, no additional parameters beyond the connector. The visual tokens sit in the KV-cache just like text tokens, so the LLM can attend to them at every layer automatically.

import torch
from transformers import LlavaNextProcessor, LlavaNextForConditionalGeneration
from PIL import Image

processor = LlavaNextProcessor.from_pretrained("llava-hf/llava-v1.6-mistral-7b-hf")
model = LlavaNextForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained(
    "llava-hf/llava-v1.6-mistral-7b-hf",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
    low_cpu_mem_usage=True
).to("cuda").eval()

image = Image.open("chart.png")

# LLaVA-NeXT uses the Mistral instruction format
# <image> is a special token replaced with visual tokens at model input
conversation = [
    {
        "role": "user",
        "content": [
            {"type": "image"},
            {"type": "text", "text": "What trend is shown in this chart?"}
        ]
    }
]
prompt = processor.apply_chat_template(conversation, add_generation_prompt=True)

inputs = processor(images=image, text=prompt, return_tensors="pt").to("cuda", torch.float16)

# Model internally:
# 1. Encode image → 576+ visual tokens via CLIP + MLP connector
# 2. Replace the <image> placeholder in the text token sequence with visual tokens
# 3. Run combined sequence through Mistral-7B self-attention
with torch.no_grad():
    output = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=200, do_sample=False)

response = processor.decode(output[0], skip_special_tokens=True)
print(response)
Concatenation's main cost is token count. With a 336px / 14px patch size, a single image occupies 576 tokens. At LLaVA-NeXT's high-resolution tiling (up to 5 tiles), a single image can use
tokens, leaving around 5,000 tokens for text in a typical 8K-context window. Processing 10 images fills the entire context with visual tokens, leaving no room for text.

This is why models like BLIP-2 use a Q-Former to compress visual tokens: 32 fixed-length output tokens per image means 10 images cost only 320 tokens, enabling long multi-image conversations. The trade-off is lossy compression: fine-grained spatial details in the image may not survive Q-Former's bottleneck.

Flamingo (Alayrac et al. 2022) introduced an alternative: keep the LLM's text-only self-attention completely unchanged, and add new cross-attention layers interleaved between each transformer block. These cross-attention layers take the image features as keys and values, and let the LLM's text hidden states attend to visual information at every layer.

The original LLM weights are frozen entirely. Only the new cross-attention layers and a Perceiver resampler (which compresses image patch tokens to 64 fixed latents) are trained. This is the "gating" design: each cross-attention output is multiplied by a learnable scalar
initialised to zero, so at the start of training the added layers have no effect and the LLM behaves identically to its pre-trained state.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class GatedCrossAttention(nn.Module):
    """
    Flamingo-style gated cross-attention layer added between LLM blocks.
    Text hidden states attend to frozen visual features.
    """
    def __init__(self, text_dim=4096, visual_dim=1024, heads=16):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm_q = nn.LayerNorm(text_dim)
        self.cross_attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(
            text_dim, heads, batch_first=True
        )
        self.visual_proj = nn.Linear(visual_dim, text_dim)  # project visual dim to text dim
        # Gating: starts at 0 → no visual influence at init
        self.alpha = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(1))
        self.ff = nn.Sequential(
            nn.LayerNorm(text_dim),
            nn.Linear(text_dim, text_dim * 4),
            nn.GELU(),
            nn.Linear(text_dim * 4, text_dim)
        )
        self.alpha_ff = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(1))

    def forward(self, text_hidden, visual_features):
        """
        text_hidden:    (B, T, text_dim)    — LLM text token hiddens
        visual_features: (B, K, visual_dim)  — compressed image tokens
        """
        # Project visual to text dimension
        vis = self.visual_proj(visual_features)  # (B, K, text_dim)

        # Gated cross-attention
        q = self.norm_q(text_hidden)
        attn_out, _ = self.cross_attn(q, vis, vis)
        text_hidden = text_hidden + torch.tanh(self.alpha) * attn_out

        # Gated feed-forward
        text_hidden = text_hidden + torch.tanh(self.alpha_ff) * self.ff(text_hidden)
        return text_hidden

Cross-attention injection keeps visual tokens entirely out of the text self-attention stream — visual and text tokens never mix in the main attention path. This allows the LLM's text capabilities to remain fully intact and enables images to be processed at a fixed cost (64 latents per image) regardless of resolution.
Strategy 3: Token Interleaving for Multi-Image Contexts

Both concatenation and cross-attention injection can be extended to multiple images in a single context. The key question is positioning: where in the token sequence does each image's visual tokens appear?

Token interleaving places each image's visual tokens at the position where the image appears in the narrative: between sentences, before a caption, or mid-instruction. This mirrors how a human reads a document with embedded figures — the image is encountered where it is referenced, not always at the start.

from transformers import Idefics2Processor, Idefics2ForConditionalGeneration
from PIL import Image
import torch

processor = Idefics2Processor.from_pretrained("HuggingFaceM4/idefics2-8b")
model = Idefics2ForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained(
    "HuggingFaceM4/idefics2-8b",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16
).to("cuda").eval()

img1 = Image.open("before.jpg")
img2 = Image.open("after.jpg")

# Interleaved: each image is placed at the position it's referenced in the text
messages = [
    {
        "role": "user",
        "content": [
            {"type": "text", "text": "Here is the scene before the renovation:"},
            {"type": "image"},          # img1 tokens inserted here
            {"type": "text", "text": "And here is the same room after:"},
            {"type": "image"},          # img2 tokens inserted here
            {"type": "text", "text": "List three specific differences you observe."}
        ]
    }
]

text = processor.apply_chat_template(messages, add_generation_prompt=True)
inputs = processor(
    text=text, images=[img1, img2], return_tensors="pt"
).to("cuda", torch.float16)

with torch.no_grad():
    output = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=200)
print(processor.decode(output[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

Each model defines a specific token template for its multimodal input. Using the wrong template — or the correct template with the wrong chat format — typically produces degraded or hallucinated outputs. The
method in Hugging Face processors handles this automatically, but it is important to understand the underlying structure.

# LLaVA-1.5 (Vicuna instruction format)
# The <image> special token is replaced by visual tokens during model.forward()
LLAVA_TEMPLATE = """A chat between a curious user and an artificial intelligence assistant.
The assistant gives helpful, detailed, and polite answers to the user's questions.
USER: <image>\n{question} ASSISTANT:"""

# BLIP-2 OPT (no special image token; Q-Former output prepended directly)
BLIP2_TEMPLATE = """{question}"""  # Q-Former tokens prepended automatically

# PaliGemma
# Image tokens (256) appear first, then BOS, then the task prefix
PALIGEMMA_TEMPLATE = "{task_prefix}\n"  # e.g. "caption en\n", "answer en {question}\n"

# Idefics2 / Mistral chat format with interleaved images
# processor.apply_chat_template handles this

# Inspecting what the processor produces
from transformers import LlavaNextProcessor
processor = LlavaNextProcessor.from_pretrained("llava-hf/llava-v1.6-mistral-7b-hf")

conversation = [{"role": "user", "content": [{"type": "image"}, {"type": "text", "text": "Describe."}]}]
formatted = processor.apply_chat_template(conversation, add_generation_prompt=True)
print(formatted)
# [INST] <image>\nDescribe. [/INST]

Regardless of the fusion strategy, training a multimodal LLM follows a two-stage (sometimes three-stage) curriculum designed to preserve the LLM's language capabilities while teaching it to interpret visual input.

: only the connector (MLP, Q-Former, or cross-attention layers) is trained. The vision encoder and LLM are frozen. The task is image-caption pairs from CC3M, LAION-CC-SBU, or similar datasets. The objective is to teach the connector to project visual tokens into a space the frozen LLM can meaningfully process. This stage is fast (1–3 hours on 8 GPUs) and cheap.

: the connector and (optionally) the LLM are trained together on instruction-following data: VQA datasets, multi-turn conversations about images (LLaVA-Instruct-150K, ShareGPT4V), grounding tasks, and document understanding pairs. The LLM learns to follow instructions about visual content rather than just captioning images.

: some models add a third stage targeting specific capabilities: chart and document understanding (tiled encoding), medical imaging, OCR, or code generation from screenshots. This stage uses smaller, more targeted datasets and often fine-tunes all components end-to-end.

# Minimal Stage 1 training loop (connector-only)
import torch
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader

def train_stage1(
    vision_encoder,   # frozen
    connector,        # trainable
    llm,              # frozen
    dataloader,       # (image, caption) pairs
    optimizer,
    epochs=1
):
    """Align connector: images → visual tokens the LLM can interpret."""
    vision_encoder.eval()
    llm.eval()
    connector.train()

    for epoch in range(epochs):
        for images, captions in dataloader:
            # 1. Encode images (no grad through encoder)
            with torch.no_grad():
                vis_out = vision_encoder(pixel_values=images)
            patch_tokens = vis_out.last_hidden_state[:, 1:]  # drop CLS

            # 2. Project through connector (grad flows here)
            visual_embeds = connector(patch_tokens)           # (B, N, llm_dim)

            # 3. Tokenise captions
            text_ids = llm.tokenizer(captions, return_tensors="pt",
                                     padding=True, truncation=True).input_ids
            text_embeds = llm.get_input_embeddings()(text_ids)

            # 4. Concatenate and compute LM loss on caption tokens
            # Visual embeds are prepended; loss is masked to caption tokens only
            combined = torch.cat([visual_embeds, text_embeds[:, :-1]], dim=1)
            labels = torch.full(
                (text_ids.size(0), visual_embeds.size(1)), -100  # ignore visual
            )
            labels = torch.cat([labels, text_ids[:, 1:]], dim=1)

            with torch.no_grad():
                lm_out = llm(inputs_embeds=combined, labels=labels)
            loss = lm_out.loss  # LM loss only on caption tokens

            optimizer.zero_grad(); loss.backward(); optimizer.step()
        print(f"Epoch {epoch}: loss={loss.item():.4f}")
Comparison: When to Use Which Strategy
Token concatenation dominates because it's simpler and works well

: prepending visual tokens to the text sequence requires no changes to the LLM architecture, yet the LLM's self-attention automatically learns to attend to visual tokens. LLaVA-1.5's 2-layer MLP + concatenation approach outperforms many more complex designs.
Cross-attention injection is the token-efficient choice

: Flamingo-style gated cross-attention keeps visual tokens out of the text's self-attention context window, enabling hundreds of images per conversation — critical for long-document or video use cases.
Gated initialisation is critical for cross-attention injection
to zero at initialisation ensures the frozen LLM is unaffected at the start of training and language capabilities are preserved as visual learning begins.
: every multimodal LLM has a specific input format (where the
token appears, what chat format surrounds it). Using the wrong template consistently produces degraded results. Always use the model's published
Two-stage training is the standard recipe

: first align the connector on image-caption pairs (connector trainable, everything else frozen), then fine-tune on instruction-following data (connector + LLM, possibly LoRA). The two stages serve different purposes and should not be collapsed into one.
